issue_title: $RESPONSE and json_response_paths fail if content-type isn't JSON
issue_body:
Probably a feature request ...

Right now, our API supports XML and JSON and we'd ideally love to just toggle between the two. 

Because of the way gabbi does the response_strings, and using $ENVIRON in the defaults: for content-type/accept, we can so almost pull this off.

Two places where the fancy idea fails:
1. Any use of $RESPONSE with an XML content-type response will result in:
```
----------------------------------------------------------------------
_StringException: Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/gabbi/case.py", line 78, in wrapper
    func(self)
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/gabbi/case.py", line 118, in test_request
    self._run_test()
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/gabbi/case.py", line 291, in _run_test
    base_url = self.replace_template(test['url'])
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/gabbi/case.py", line 132, in replace_template
    message = getattr(self, method)(message)

    self._json_replacer, message)
  File "/System/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/re.py", line 151, in sub
    return _compile(pattern, flags).sub(repl, string, count)
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/gabbi/case.py", line 202, in _json_replacer
    return str(self.extract_json_path_value(self.prior.json_data, path))

2. `json_response_paths:` can't be used and ignored when XML or non-JSON is detected. It would be lovely if this was possible. It'd be nice to get some "bonus" JSON tests when JSON is detected, and ignored with a warning otherwise.

issue_title: module design: why not use python submodules
issue_body:
Pylama is a great tool but to manage all those submodules separately seems a little bit unmanageable in my opinion.

Why not add a `./submodules` folder at root and then symlink used files to those modules?

This would make the module almost always up to date.

issue_title: Raising zero to a large power mismatch with Python long
issue_body:
Python long gives 0 for (0 ** n) for any n, no matter how large.  mpz in gmpy2 raises a ValueError (outrageous exponent) if n is too large.  Can cause some issues if assuming complete drop-in replacement of mpz for Python long.

`>>> import gmpy2`
`>>> from gmpy2 import mpz`
`>>> mpz(0) ** (mpz(100) ** mpz(100))`
`Traceback (most recent call last):`
`  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>`
`ValueError: pow() outrageous exponent`
`>>> 0 ** (100 ** 100)`
`0L`

issue_title: Inefficient packet writing
issue_body:
Each packet write checks the system endianness, and constructs an LE/PktHdr, to allow the packet to be written. 

I am not aware of conditions under which system endianness could change between packet writes, so this testing should be moved to the Writer initialization instead.

issue_body:
Hello, and thanks for all the work you've done on this!

I am building a script that will query comment data per each ticket id made in the last hour, including comment text, author, and time.  I see the wrapper can utilize ticket audits, and am not sure if that's the better way to go or if you have end points for comment text, author, and time that would be queryable on their own.

My current script is as follows, I hope to just flesh it out to include the comment data described above.  Any input you may have would be very helpful.  Thank you!!

```python

import zenpy, datetime, zdcreds

# Authenticate with Zendesk client
zenpy_client = zenpy.Zenpy(**zdcreds.creds)

# Reads end time of last export, to be used as start time for new export
with open("export_time.txt","r") as f:
    export_time = f.read()

result_generator = zenpy_client.tickets.incremental(start_time=int(export_time))

# Pulls ticket data and manually formats into SQL syntax
# Output columns are ordered as follows: platform, player_id, location, ticket_id, ticket_tags, group, ticket_created, last_update
custom = ''
data = ''
for ticket in result_generator:
    for field in ticket.custom_fields:
        if ((field['id'] == 29655218) or (field['id'] == 22917550) or (field['id'] == 43800168)):
            custom = custom+'\"'+str(field['value'])+'\", '
    data = data+'('+custom+'\"'+str(ticket.id)+'\", \"'+str(ticket.tags)+'\", \"'+str(ticket.group_id)+'\", '+'STR_TO_DATE(\''+str(ticket.created_at)+'\', \'%Y-%m-%dT%H:%i:%sZ\')'+', '+'STR_TO_DATE(\''+str(ticket.updated_at)+'\', \'%Y-%m-%dT%H:%i:%sZ\')),'+'\n'
    custom = ''
data = data[:-2]

# Writes end time of export to text file for use as start time of future export
with open("export_time.txt","w+") as f:
    f.write(str(result_generator.end_time))
```

issue_title: Support FreeBSD (currently - RuntimeError no suitable implementation)
issue_body:
Relevant code in similar library: https://github.com/gavinbeatty/python-monotonic-time/blob/master/monotonic_time.py.in#L59

Boils down to:
* Linux `clock_gettime(1, &ts)`
* FreeBSD `clock_gettime(4, &ts)`

where 4 is also `#define CLOCK_MONOTONIC	4` in https://github.com/freebsd/freebsd/blob/master/include/time.h

Please, add support for FreeBSD.

issue_title: pcap2dev.py does not support encrypted .pcap traffic
issue_body:
In order to simulate a large switch, we have captured a `pcap` file of an `snmpbulkwalk` of (almost) an entire switch. However, this was done using `SNMPv3` with `-l authPriv`, so the resulting `pcap` file contains encrypted SNMP traffic.

It appears that `pcap2dev.py` has no means to decrypt such a `pcap` file. Is this possible?

(and no, it was not possible to use `snmprec.py` to bulk-walk the switch; the host from which we walked the switch does not have `snmpsim` installed, and we cannot install it there for reasons).

issue_title: Insufficient warning when an estimation method with low accuracy is used
issue_body:
@ @Currently the library provides no warning when an estimation method is used to calculate a chemical property. This was exemplified in #3 , where glycerol has no accurate viscosity data at the time and the estimation methods Letsou_Stiel and Przedziecki_Sridhar worked terribly but without any warning. 

It might be a good idea to provide a warning when an inaccurate estimation method is used, or to provide an overall description of the quality of the data for a chemical, or to attempt to quantify the error of a property.

It would be straightforward to generate a warning, using the warnings module, whenever a low-accuracy estimation method is called. However, how often should they be generated? If a plotting command is used and the estimation method is used repeatedly, generating a warning for each calculation would not be desirable. Furthermore, some caching is used to store chemical properties for future use. The caching layer does not know what method was used, and if it is accurate or not.

One more pain point - Many of the VDI PPDS coefficients are regressed from estimated data using the same methods used in the library. The same applies for what DIPPR data are available. Even the TRC ideal gas heat capacity database contains plenty of estimated data using the Benson method. None of these sources state which chemicals have estimated data and which do not. Some coefficient sources, such as the VDI PPDS coefficients, do not even have a temperature range specified, and definitely stop working at very low and very temperatures - some crude measurements based on properties derivatives are currently used to limit these coefficients.

The long-term solution is to do what NIST's Thermodynamics Research Center does - collect experimental data, regress coefficients, and estimate uncertainties based on that. 

Considering the above, there is no obvious path for when to generate warnings. Please share any thoughts - use cases, issues when you were surprised by a low-accuracy datum, or other suggestions about providing warnings to the user here.

issue_title: classmethod: no completion on instance of cls
Jedi locks pyd files preventing recompilation
issue_body:
If you are developing an extension module for Python and build it in-place (Or use setup.py develop/editable install which also does this) and than your editor requests completions for something involving your built extension module, Jedi will load in the completion process it which will prevent it from being rebuilt on Windows (Extension modules can't be unloaded).

To reproduce:
1. Download [hello_mod.zip](https://github.com/davidhalter/jedi/files/2337441/hello_mod.zip).
2. Build it in-place: `python setup.py build_ext --inplace`.
3. Run `python test.py`. (Requires `jedi`, of course, and `six`).
4. Try to rebuild the module `python setup.py build_ext --inplace`.
5. You will get the following error:
    ```cmd
    C:\hello_mod>python setup.py build_ext --inplace
    running build_ext
    copying build\lib.win-amd64-3.7\_hello.cp37-win_amd64.pyd ->
    error: could not delete '_hello.cp37-win_amd64.pyd': Access is denied
    ```

Originally found here https://github.com/Microsoft/vscode-python/issues/2458

cc @brettcannon

@client.event

@client.event

issue_title: Performance degrades for synchronous producer in 1.2.0 vs 0.9.4
issue_body:
Hi!

Wikimedia been using (mumrah's) kafka-python 0.9.4 for our [EventLogging](https://github.com/wikimedia/eventlogging) codebase.  We use pykafka for consuming, mainly because it had a BalancedConsumer before kafka-python ever did.  We recently upgraded our brokers to 0.9.  We've also been having an issue with pykafka where consumer processes can die during broker restarts.  All of this is making us consider switching to kafka-python.

However, one of our use cases is synchronous production.  We have an HTTP Kafka produce service that uses kafka-python 0.9.4.  We only want to return a 200 response to clients if we are 100% sure that Kafka has persisted the message.  In 0.9.4, this could be accomplished by setting `async=False` and `req_acks=-1`.

kafka-python 1.2.0 provides both the deprecated `SimpleProducer` and the new `KafkaProducer` interfaces.  With both producers, we see a significant performance regression when doing 'synchronous' production.  With 0.9.4 we could synchronously produce in 1-10ms.  1.2.0 takes at about an order of magnitude longer.

#### SimpleProducer
For `SimpleProducer`, the regression seems to be mostly caused by some significant changes to `SimpleClient` since 0.9.4. [Previously](https://github.com/dpkp/kafka-python/blob/v0.9.4/kafka/client.py#L175), `_send_broker_aware_request` would iterate through each payload produce request and receive a response.  In the current version, it sends each payload produce request asynchronously, and then iterates while waiting for all futures to resolve. I used line_profiler to profile the `_send_broker_aware_request` in 0.9.4 and 1.2.0.  Over 1000 individual produce requests, in 0.9.4, `conn.recv()` was called 1000 times.  However, in 1.2.0, `conn.recv()` was called 575283 times.  I'm aware that `conn` is a different type of connection in each version (`BrokerConnection` `KafkaConnection`) and that `conn.recv()` is doing different things.  I might be way off here, but it seems the constant looping over a non blocking is causing a big performance hit.  Performance is about an order of magnitude worse in 1.2.0.

My line_profiler results are here: https://gist.github.com/ottomata/741ccf084f220249102d6c97abda963d

#### KafkaProducer
Ok, buuut, I should use the new `KafkaProducer`, right?  I should!  I would much prefer to do this, as we would like to eventually take advantage of some newer Kafka features.  But I need a way to do 'synchronous' production.

I've tried by setting `batch_size=0`, and doing `response.get()` to wait for my produce request future to resolve.  This has a huge performance hit, also of about an order of magnitude.  I've tried to dive into the `KafkaProducer` code a bit here, but got a little lost.  In doing so I noticed a some places where futures seemed to be waited for just by looping continuously.  If produce throughput is high enough, the constant iteration and checking for future resolution wouldn't be noticed, but in our 'synchronous' use case, we want to produce 1 message at a time.  I don't yet have a full grasp on this code, so I'm probably just missing something simple.

Is there a way to quickly produce a single message at a time?

       break

Which is a bit tedious.

issue_title: Support redirecting to HTTPS / setting HSTS header
issue_body:
Typically redirecting HTTP requests to HTTPS or setting an HSTS header would be the responsibility of ngnix/Apache, however when using Heroku these aren't an option.

One might say that the wgsi app that WhiteNoise wraps should handle this instead ([example](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.8/ref/settings/#std:setting-SECURE_SSL_REDIRECT)) -- since it could make the redirect when serving the homepage. 

However:
(a) this doesn't protect direct requests to static assets served by WhiteNoise (even if HSTS was enabled, the first visit to the site might be to a WhiteNoise served file instead)
(b) this doesn't help sites whose homepage is not dynamic, and is served by WhiteNoise itself (which is the case I hit, since the site UI is entirely Angular.js, with Django powering just the site API).

It's worth noting that when determining whether to redirect on Heroku, the `HTTP_X_FORWARDED_PROTO` header must be observed, since both HTTP and HTTPS requests arrive on the same port on the Heroku web dyno. (And similarly for Azure, one must check `x-arr-ssl`.)

I see three options:
1) Add support for redirects + HSTS in WhiteNoise.
2) Add an option for only serving requests over HTTPS via WhiteNoise, and let anything else fall through to the wrapped wgsi app so it can respond with the redirect/HSTS header itself.
3) Wontfix for WhiteNoise, but add recommendations to the docs for using some other wgsi wrapper that itself wraps WhiteNoise (though I'm struggling to find something generic).

@evansd, what are your thoughts? :-)

Dr Gerard Hammond
Cancer Group

issue_title: Long/Int Cast Issues
issue_body:
With Teradata 64 ODBC on unixODBC (Linux 12.04) running pyodbc (3.11b) and pyodbc (3.0.7) integer values are incorrectly represented:

    In [16]: cur.execute("select 1").fetchall()
    Out[16]: [(140376711102465, )]

    In [17]: cur.execute("select cast(1 as int)").fetchall()
    Out[17]: [(140376711102465, )]

In order to work I need to cast the values manually to bigint:

    In [18]: cur.execute("select cast(1 as bigint)").fetchall()
    Out[18]: [(1L, )]

Per Teradata's docs a integer type is signed 4-byte (-2.147B - 2.147B) number.  The value returned by `pyodbc` appears to far larger than that though.

Problem appears similar to: https://github.com/mkleehammer/pyodbc/issues/42

issue_title: Asynchronous modules stop processing events on SIGSTOP
issue_body:
When i3bar is hidden, the statusline generator gets a SIGSTOP signal. Thus, the asynchronous modules cannot process new events, which currently leads to https://github.com/i3/i3/issues/2280 in i3 - ultimately, this can freeze i3. Depending on how this is solved, it might be desirable to have the event processing in a different process, so it is not stopped. What do you think?

This is also mentioned in https://github.com/ultrabug/py3status/issues/179 for a different use case.

issue_title: consolidate pipeline for efficiency's sake
issue_body:
We have three goals here:

1. Remove duplicate or unnecessary computation
2. Reduce saved file sizes by removing unnecessary steps, and by reducing what DictVectorizer has to save. 
3. Avoid the quarter second penalty incurred each time we invoke `SelectFromModel.transform()`. This gets super painful at prediction time, particularly in cases where you have many subpredictors which each have their own feature selection. https://github.com/scikit-learn/scikit-learn/issues/7478

Changes to make:

- [x] Combine FeatureSelection into DictVectorizer using dv.restrict()
- [x] Reconstruct the trained subpredictor pipelines so they only have dv (which includes feature selection) and FinalModelATC (any any other super late stage things they need, but i can't think of any offhand).
- [ ]Mmove where subpredictors sit in the final pipeline. they'll have to sit at the end of all the stuff we do with dictionaries. This will avoid a lot of duplicate computation. 
- [x] Do something similar with the final pipeline: combine dv and feature selection. This might involve a little bit of logic to find what all the steps are that are included, since they don't seem to be ordered in named_steps.

issue_title: Verify that CEF library version (dll/so file) is the one that CEF Python was built with
issue_body:
It might be tempting for users to upgrade CEF version in CEF Python by downloading latest CEF from Spotify Automated builds and replacing DLL files like libcef.dll within CEF Python. Something like this is not allowed. This will result in strange errors. If user is lucky it might work for some time, but at some point he will get into troubles definietly.

Currently there is available cef.[GetVersion](https://github.com/cztomczak/cefpython/blob/master/api/cefpython.md#getversion) function that returns CEF version and CEF API hash that can be used to verify versions. These are fixed versions numbers embedded when building CEF Python from sources. However I don't see API in CEF to get api hash version from the running libcef.dll. I believe CefGlue makes such API hash checks, so it needs to be checked how it is done there.

> import iptc

Recently, i found the speed when using swifter is 5-10x slower than using vanilla pandas apply for case that the process is not vectorized (my case is doing text preprocessing).

The experiment is like this:

```python

import pandas as pd
import swifter

def clean_text(text):
    text = text.strip()
    text = text.replace(' ', '_')

N_rows = 7000000

issue_title: Python 3.4.2 pseq Incompatibility
issue_body:
Due to a bug in python 3.4.2, `pseq` is only compatible with python 3.4.4. The error occurs in many tests: `_pickle.UnpicklingError: pickle exhausted before end of frame`.

This is well described here: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/34319854/pickle-exhausted-before-end-of-frame-when-using-dill-with-code-that-works-fine

`pyfunctional` now only tests against 3.4.4 which means that officially 3.4.2 is not supported, but regardless should continue working using just `seq`.

issue_title: a different intersection operation
issue_body:
I have a big `IntervalTree` and a small `IntervalTree`. For convenience, let's call them `A` and `B`. 

I want to do a big overlap query, such that I get, for every member of B, a list of A intervals that overlap with it. `tree.search` does this for a single point or interval, so I *can* iterate one tree, I suppose, but I suspect this function exists in the library, I just can't seem to find it. 

The documentation lists `result_tree = tree1 & tree2` but I don't want a result tree, I want a list of all the intervals from `tree2` which intersect with each of the intervals in `tree1`. Presumably the other functions listed under the intersection bullet do the same thing. 

---

On another note, the documentation says: 
```
- Membership queries

interval_obj in tree (this is fastest, O(1))
tree.containsi(begin, end, data)
tree.overlaps(point)
tree.overlaps(begin, end)
```

Why is `tree.overlaps` a membership query? That seems unintuitive, when a couple bullets above, you have `Overlap queries`.

issue_title: Sub Periods don't get their parent's timezone
issue_body:
I have to say this one was a doozy, there are just too many places where dates are timezone converted. These might be related to a number of other issues.

## TL;DR

``Period`` needs to include its timezone info when requested to generate sub Periods, otherwise some recurrences rules fail because a recurrence parameters (like week day number) in a given datetime in UTC might be different from other timezones.

## Long explanation

Django stores ``start`` and ``end`` date fields of ``Event`` model in UTC, but there is no place where the original timezone is stored, for this reason the ``get_occurrences`` method (and auxiliary methods) in ``Event`` model takes the tzinfo from the start date of the parameter and localizes the dates from the models with the timezone on every request.

``Period`` class represents a timespan and is in charge of returning the occurrences that happen inside it. ``Period`` also can generate sub periods of time upon request (instances of Period). This is used when building the slots in the calendar web UI. Internally, the Period class uses normalized timezone-awared dates in UTC, and the ``tzinfo`` parameter is used to normalize and localize dates before sending them to ``Event.get_occurrences``.

### The problem
All generated sub periods are missing its parent tzinfo, so it defaults to ``UTC``. 

### Symptoms
Occurrences are generated in incorrect dates.

### Example to illustrate

* Config django to be in a timezone != UTC, in my case ``UTC-3`` ("America/Montevideo").
* Create a ``Daily`` rule that only occurs on Saturdays (``byweekno:SA`` or ``byweekno:5``)
* Create an event that starts on a Saturday at 22:00 hs, example ``Sat 2017-01-07 22:00 UYT``
* Event will be stored in model and DB as ``Sun 2017-01-08 01:00`` (UTC). Notice that the hour is now 1am in UTC, and the rule is "every Saturday".
* Get occurrences for event between ``Sat 2017-01-06 22:00 UYT`` and ``Sat 2017-01-28 23:00 UYT``. It will return the correct days but all start times will be on 1 am UTC. Later on when these datetimes get converted to the user timezone, they'll be one day early.

issue_title: Using models from another namespace? Designed my project for models to be in separate folder, to be reused one with another - end up with "no spec provided" 500 swagger.json
issue_body:
Often defining models right there with the views becomes a total mess, especially when models are reused in another namespaces.
First, **resource_model_a** becomes **resource_model_a_dict**, then, if there was another nested model inside this dict, it too becomes a dict, and then there are a whole bunch of helper functions and project files are filled with helpers to overcome "no spec provided".

What's the best way to solve this problem?


Examples:

I want to have this

models/products.py

```python
from flask_restplus import Namespace, fields

api = Namespace("ProductsNamespace")

resource_product = api.model("Product",  {
  'id': fields.Integer,
  'name': fields.String
})

```

models/owners.py

```python
from flask_restplus import Namespace, fields
from .products import resource_product

api = Namespace("OwnersNamespace")

resource_owner = api.model("Owner", {
  'id': fields.Integer,
  'name': fields.String,
  'products': fields.List(fields.Nested(resource_product))
})
```


views/owners.py

```python
from flask_restplus import Namespace, fields, Resource
from ..models.owners import resource_owner

api = Namespace("OwnersApi", path="/owners")

@api.route("")
class List(Resource):
  @api.marshal_with(resource_owner)
  def get(self):
    pass
```

issue_title: add migration steps and documentation to move to django-allauth
issue_body:
[django-allauth](https://github.com/pennersr/django-allauth) provides a more comprehensive solution to User registration and authentication than this project. Given the complexity of integrating different registration, authentication (two-factor, social auth, etc) with User management it generally makes sense to adopt one complete solution instead of combining many disparate solutions.

Ideally, django-registration should provide both code and documentation to move a Django project to django-allauth in a tested, repeatable way.

issue_title: Make templatetags more generally usable
issue_body:
Zinnia's [template tags](/Fantomas42/django-blog-zinnia/blob/develop/zinnia/templatetags/zinnia.py#L52) are very useful for customized solutions. Though, some limitations are maybe unnecessary and could be removed.

We found this as we were integrating Zinnia into another web project.

## More generic context

The functions `get_categories`, `get_categories_tree`, `get_authors` have an additional `context_category` and `context_author`. Unfortunately, `category` and `author` are not always available in the context, e.g. in a blog entry detail. (In this case the `context_*` value is `None`, of course.)

The singular of the words `context_author` and `context_category` suggests that the tags are designed for the following use cases:

1. Highlight the currently visited category (or author page) in a list of categories or authors.
1. Show a list of all published, non-empty categories (or authors) anywhere, no highlighting of any item implied.

However, there is an additional, maybe not too uncommon use case that is not yet covered:

* Show a list of categories (or authors) on a blog **entry detail** ~~or blog **entry list** page~~,
* and highlight **all categories** (or authors) used in the entry ~~or entry list~~.

The values missing in the context for this use case can be provided by:
```
    try:
        entry_categories = context.get('object').categories.all()
    except AttributeError:  # no object in the context
        entry_categories = None
```

There may be other, similar use cases also for the other tags.

issue_title: self._timeout is used in the TimeoutError exception even if it has been change in self.timeout
issue_body:
```
In [27]: api = overpass.API(timeout=3)

In [28]: response = api.Get(s)
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
TimeoutError                              Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-28-cee6cde6e022> in <module>()
----> 1 response = api.Get(s)

/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/overpass/api.py in Get(self, query, responseformat, verbosity)
     43 
     44         # Get the response from Overpass
---> 45         raw_response = self._GetFromOverpass(full_query)
     46 
     47         if responseformat == "xml":

/usr/lib/python3/dist-packages/overpass/api.py in _GetFromOverpass(self, query)
    100 
    101         except requests.exceptions.Timeout:
--> 102             raise TimeoutError(self._timeout)
    103 
    104         self._status = r.status_code

TimeoutError: 25
```

that's because it uses ```self._timeout``` (always set to 25) but the API class defines the timeout as

```self.timeout = kwargs.get("timeout", self._timeout)```

and it's the correct value used to determine if it has timed out or not, so i think that's the value to be used in the exception as well

issue_title: hashivault_token_create does not work with authtype: approle
issue_body:
I'm currently testing this hashivault module for python, but I stumbled accross the following error:

I'm trying to generate a token for a defined approle, for this I'm retrieving the role_id and secret_id using the provided methods. According to the documentation the following should then retrieve the token:

```yaml
---
- name: Test Vault lookup
  hosts: localhost
  no_log: false
  tasks:
  - hashivault_approle_role_id:
      name: ansible
    register: 'vault_role_id'

  - debug:
      msg: "{{ vault_role_id.id }}"

  - hashivault_approle_role_secret_create:
      name: ansible
    register: 'vault_role_secret_create'

  - debug:
      msg: "{{ vault_role_secret_create.secret_id }}"

  - hashivault_token_create:
      display_name: approle
      authtype: approle
      role_id: "{{ vault_role_id.id }}"
      secret_id: "{{ vault_role_secret_create.secret_id }}"
      policies: [ "ansible" ]
    register: vault_token

  - debug:
      msg: "{{ vault_token }}"

  environment:
    VAULT_ADDR: "https://localhost:8200"
    VAULT_CACERT: "cert"
    VAULT_TOKEN: fancy_token
```

The second request is running against auth/token/create, which fails, as my policy does not allow this. I think the problem is related to the call of create_token [here](https://github.com/TerryHowe/ansible-modules-hashivault/blob/77aa207583130b7dac0454f6244beb2f0576ce85/ansible/modules/hashivault/hashivault_token_create.py#L154) as this will be executed after the [AppRoleClient call](https://github.com/TerryHowe/ansible-modules-hashivault/blob/77aa207583130b7dac0454f6244beb2f0576ce85/ansible/module_utils/hashivault.py#L67). 

I guess this bug applies to the other authtypes as well, as the create_token function is called afterwards again and the [hashivault_auth](https://github.com/TerryHowe/ansible-modules-hashivault/blob/77aa207583130b7dac0454f6244beb2f0576ce85/ansible/module_utils/hashivault.py#L52) method is already requesting the tokens.

except mx.MXNetError:
   print('No problem.')
```

## Steps to reproduce
1. Run any function that can fail with an exception and catch it
2. You will notice that output is being written, including the stacktrace

## What have you tried to solve it?

I tried to redirect stderr in python while calling the function like so:

`sys.stderr = open(os.devnull)`

I also tried redirecting stderr on commandline level:

`$ ./my_script.py 2> /dev/null

This works, but also silences all other output onto stderr, including uncaught exceptions and the helptext from pythons argument parser.

This is extremely annoying when trying to write a command-line tool based on mxnet, as it drowns out relevant ouput.

def euler2mat(rot):
     roll = mx.sym.slice_axis(rot, axis=1, begin=0, end=1)

     yaw = mx.sym.slice_axis(rot, axis=1, begin=2, end=3)

     # Expand to B x 1 x 1
     roll = mx.sym.expand_dims(roll, axis=2)
     pitch = mx.sym.expand_dims(pitch, axis=2)
     yaw = mx.sym.expand_dims(yaw, axis=2)
     zeros = mx.sym.zeros_like(roll)
     ones  = mx.sym.ones_like(roll)

     cosz = mx.sym.cos(yaw)
     sinz = mx.sym.sin(yaw)
     yaw_mat_1 = mx.sym.concat(cosz, -1 * sinz, zeros, dim=2)
     yaw_mat_2 = mx.sym.concat(sinz,  cosz, zeros, dim=2)
     yaw_mat_3 = mx.sym.concat(zeros, zeros, ones, dim=2)
     yaw_mat = mx.sym.concat(yaw_mat_1, yaw_mat_2, yaw_mat_3, dim=1)

     cosy = mx.sym.cos(pitch)
     siny = mx.sym.sin(pitch)
     pitch_mat_1 = mx.sym.concat(cosy, zeros, siny, dim=2)
     pitch_mat_2 = mx.sym.concat(zeros, ones, zeros, dim=2)
     pitch_mat_3 = mx.sym.concat(-1 * siny, zeros, cosy, dim=2)
     pitch_mat = mx.sym.concat(pitch_mat_1, pitch_mat_2, pitch_mat_3, dim=1)

     # Expand to B x 3 x 3
     cosx = mx.sym.cos(roll)
     sinx = mx.sym.sin(roll)
     roll_mat_1 = mx.sym.concat(ones, zeros, zeros, dim=2)
     roll_mat_2 = mx.sym.concat(zeros, cosx, -1 * sinx, dim=2)
     roll_mat_3 = mx.sym.concat(zeros, sinx, cosx, dim=2)
     roll_mat = mx.sym.concat(roll_mat_1, roll_mat_2, roll_mat_3, dim=1)
     rotMat = mx.sym.batch_dot(yaw_mat, pitch_mat)
     rotMat = mx.sym.batch_dot(rotMat, roll_mat)

     return rotMat

if __name__=='__main__':
     pose = mx.sym.Variable('pose')
     mat = euler2mat(pose)
     loss = mx.sym.MakeLoss(data = mx.sym.sum(mat, axis=[1,2]),
             grad_scale=1.0)

     pose_np = mx.nd.array(np.ones([1, 3]))
     executor = loss.simple_bind(ctx=mx.cpu(), pose=(1,3))
     executor.forward(is_train=True, pose=pose_np)
     print executor.outputs

     executor.backward()
     print executor.grad_arrays

````
## Steps to reproduce
1. run the code, 

when I remove the batch_dot, everything goes find, or I just use batch_dot for two matrix, it seems also ok. When I jointly use the two. The program crashes.

I am not sure what is the reason. Anything thought on this ?

issue_title: Let DIct() accept a Mapping as value (and probably other traitlets their ABC counterparts)
issue_body:
It seems that currently the traitlet for `Dict` only allows for a real `dict`, but not a Mapping:

https://github.com/ipython/traitlets/blob/master/traitlets/traitlets.py#L2619

```python
     # klass=dict means that each new value will be tested for `isinstance(new_val, dict)
     super(Dict, self).__init__(klass=dict, args=args, **kwargs)
``` 

This means that I can't add a subclass of type `Mapping` to the trait

```python
class MyMap(MutableMapping):
    def __delitem__(self, name):
        pass
    def __getitem__(self, name):
        pass
    def __iter__(self):
        pass
    def __len__(self):
        pass
    def __setitem__(self, name, obj):
        pass
Dict().validate(obj="whatever", value=MyMap())

TraitError: The 'None' trait of a whatever instance must be a dict, but a value of class 'str' (i.e. '') was specified.
```

It would be nice if the traitlets could work with their collections abc counterpart...

I my case I want to pass my own [implementation of a dict](https://github.com/Cadair/jupyter_environment_kernels/blob/master/environment_kernels/lazyobj.py) as [a 'env' into a kernel spec](https://github.com/Cadair/jupyter_environment_kernels/blob/master/environment_kernels/envs_common.py#L53-L62). 

Up to now I put [`dict` as a parent class](https://github.com/Cadair/jupyter_environment_kernels/blob/master/environment_kernels/lazyobj.py#L39) to get around the traitlet check. But this now doesn't work anymore as `dict.update(mapping)` seems to take a few shortcuts if mapping is a subclass of dict. In this case it seems that none of the python functions from the mapping are called and I end up with a unupdated dict. This burns me in https://github.com/jupyter/jupyter_client/blob/master/jupyter_client/manager.py#L252 where the activated environment doesn't get applied (this worked in older python version, but not anymore in the py35 or py36).

issue_title: Using RangeDailyBase with dummy task fails with "Unfulfilled dependencies"
issue_body:
When trying to use the RangeDailyBase class as described in [Triggering recurring tasks](http://luigi.readthedocs.org/en/stable/luigi_patterns.html#triggering-recurring-tasks) with a dummy task that triggers several tasks with a date parameter, it fails at the end of a run with the following error:

```
RuntimeError: Unfulfilled dependencies at run time: UpdateRedshiftTables(date=2015-07-23), UpdateRedshiftTables(date=2015-07-24), UpdateRedshiftTables(date=2015-07-25)
```

The command I used is:

```
luigi --module update_redshift --local-scheduler RangeDailyBase --of UpdateRedshiftTables --start 2015-07-23
```

The dummy task looks like this (pretty much the exact same setup as in the docs):

```
class UpdateRedshiftTables(luigi.Task):
    date = luigi.DateParameter()

    def requires(self):
        yield UpdateRedshiftTableA(date=self.date)
        yield UpdateRedshiftTableB(date=self.date)
```

It looks to me like RangeDailyBase expects the task it runs to have an output. Is there a workaround for this? Do I need to give `UpdateRedshiftTables` a dummy output to the filesystem for this to work?

Also: I've checked this with the individual tasks (which have outputs), and they complete successfully with no errors when run with RangeDailyBase. I can also run the `UpdateRedshiftTables` task directly for a single date with no errors.

issue_title: RemoteTarget creates a non-existant file/directory, but its test expects a raised error
issue_body:
Hey folks -

I was taking a look at some tests, when I saw that on Travis, one particular test that is testing `luigi/contrib/ssh.py` [fails but the build still succeeds](https://travis-ci.org/spotify/luigi/jobs/101167651#L308). I thought I'd fix this, since I assumed that creating a temp file in `/etc` was the issue by just using Python's `tempfile` lib.

However, digging through `luigi/contrib/ssh.py:RemoteTarget`, it looks like the creation of the `file_writer` creates the parents' directories automatically (and then for the actual file to be created if it's not already there). So that leads me to be a bit confused that the associated test [luigi/contrib/test_ssh.py](https://github.com/spotify/luigi/blob/master/test/contrib/test_ssh.py#L195-L200) expects `RemoteCalledProcessError` to be raised, when it's actually not.

Which would be the preferred behavior?

issue_title: Crash when loading MTL/OBJ + (fix / workaround)
GLTF exporter: materials missing technique attribute
issue_body:
Exported GLTFs' materials are missing a "technique" attribute, and according to the spec such materials should be rendered simply using some plain gray default material:
https://github.com/KhronosGroup/glTF/tree/master/specification/1.0#materials-and-shading

It's a bit of a pain, because AFAIK even the simplest technique requires a vertex and fragment shader to be defined, but we could define such a technique and include it with each exported material.

issue_title: Bug when request param matches non typed logic function param
issue_body:
If you logic function has a non-docotor-typed annotation (or no annotation) and a request parameter matches that, it will attempt to coerce the parameter.

For example:

```python
def get_foo(id: FooID, use_cache: bool = False):
    # ...
```

If you pass use_cache in the query string `/foo/1?use_cache=1` it will cause an error that looks something like:

issue_title: Firehose client `generate_presigned_url` method throws exception
issue_body:
When trying to generate a presigned URL for a firehose `put_record` operation, the following exception is thrown:

    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "foo.py", line 17, in <module>
        'Data': b'FOOBARBAZQUX'
      File "[...]/boto3/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/botocore/signers.py", line 517, in generate_presigned_url
        operation_name=operation_name)
      File "[...]/boto3/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/botocore/signers.py", line 253, in generate_presigned_url
        'presign-url', expires_in)
      File "[...]/boto3/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/botocore/signers.py", line 147, in sign
        auth.add_auth(request)
      File "[...]/boto3/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/botocore/auth.py", line 319, in add_auth
        self._modify_request_before_signing(request)
      File "[...]/boto3/venv/lib/python3.4/site-packages/botocore/auth.py", line 459, in _modify_request_before_signing
        query_dict.update(request.data)
    TypeError: cannot convert dictionary update sequence element #0 to a sequence

The following code demonstrates the issue:

    import boto3
    
    kinesis = boto3.client(
        'firehose',
        region_name='us-east-1',
        aws_access_key_id ='KEY',
        aws_secret_access_key='SECRET'
    )
    
    kinesis.generate_presigned_url(
        'put_record',
        Params={
            'DeliveryStreamName':'STREAM',
            'Record': {
                'Data': b'FOOBARBAZQUX'
            }
        }
    )

It appears as if the request body is being converted to a bytestring via `serialize_to_request` (https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/serialize.py#L307). However, when the presigning happens later, the request body is assumed to be a dictionary  `request_signer.generate_presigned_url` (https://github.com/boto/botocore/blob/develop/botocore/auth.py#L459)

issue_title: Pre-fetch method
issue_body: When working with large amounts of data, it'd be nice to have fetch continue to pull records in another thread. For example,  cursor.prefetchmany(100000) would return 100k rows on the first call, then spawn a new thread to fetch the next 100k rows.

issue_title: SQLAlchemy/Hive connect to Spark Thrift Server (based on HiveThriftServer2)
issue_body:
Hi, 

I was trying to connect to a Spark HiveThriftServer2 on Spark 2.1.0 and https://github.com/airbnb/superset with its SQLLab tool which is sqlalchemy based. While running queries with sqlalchemy works fine, superset uses among other things get_schema_names in sqlalchemy_hive.py. I realised that Spark returns a slightly different result for SHOW SCHEMAS. It uses databaseName instead of database_name as column name, the later being expected in sqlalchemy_hive.py.

I was able to make it work with a small modification which should work for Hive, too (see below) and I was wondering if there is a chance to bring that in?

```
def get_schema_names(self, connection, **kw):
        # Equivalent to SHOW DATABASES
        return [row[0] for row in connection.execute('SHOW SCHEMAS')]
```

There are a few other problems when superset tries to get table names (index of name different) and column definitions (with get_columns) for Spark temporary views, but that is because Spark does not seem to consistently work with schemas/dbs. For example 'SHOW TABLES IN myschema' would show the temporary view but 'DESCRIBE myschmea.myview' would produce an error. I haven't tested if it is the same for persisted tables. Anyway that is a spark bug.

I realise that you are probably not aiming to support the Spark Thrift server, but having this small change in would actually help me a lot and not break Hive compatibility. Should you be interested in implementing a sqlalchemy Spark dialect on the other hand, I'd be happy to get involved.

issue_title: Create new keyword "Element Text Should Not Be"
issue_body:
I need to check if some part of the page has a text or is empty. For me there should be 2 logical ways to do that:

| Element Text Should Not Be | ${EMPTY} |
|---------------------------:|----------|

| Element Text Should Not Be Empty |
|---------------------------------:|

I think both keywords should exist in this Library

issue_title: HTML5 specialized text fields not recognized as text fields
issue_body:
HTML5 introduced several new input types, several of which are specialized text inputs including email,  number, url, search, and tel. These input types are not recognized by `Page Should Contain Textfield`, `Page Should Not Contain Textfield`, `Textfield Should Contain`,  or `Textfield Value Should Be`.
The cause is that these keywords use the constraint 'text field' and ElementFinder will filter out any input elements that do not have type 'text'. Since these inputs are still text inputs, they should be treated as such. The number type may render a spinbox control in some browsers, but still behaves like a text-type input.

Here is a test that should pass, but fails with:
Value of text field 'name=email' should have been 'selenium@xxx.yyy' but was 'None'

```
Open Browser    http://www.the-art-of-web.com/html/html5-form-validation/   gc
Input Text  name=email  selenium@xxx.yyy
Textfield Value Should Be   name=email  selenium@xxx.yyy
```

With minor modifications to elementfinder, I was able to get the above to pass.

This is being entered as a result of this: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/34169390/how-to-find-value-of-email-field-using-selenium2library

issue_title: Add Optional Unique Domain Tracking
issue_body:
It would be nice to have the option to get a list of all domains visited during testing. By having this feature in the Selenium2Library we could make keywords like `Delete All Cookies` more useful. Currently `Delete All Cookies` will only remove cookies from the current domain. It would be much more useful if we could delete all the cookies from any domain visited during testing. This ability would allow for easier browser cleanup in between tests and reduce the need to restart the browser during testing.

`Library  Selenium2Library  domain_tracking=True` (domain_tracking will default to False)

I currently have a working implementation of this feature. Some things that I am unsure of is if storing the domains in a set is too specific. Should we just store all URL's visited in a list during testing instead? I am currently parsing the URL's into domains and adding them to a set. This means that you would only get unique domains visited during testing. This seems more useful for deleting cookies, but I could see use cases for wanting all of the URLS visited. Perhaps we could add both or leave the unique domains up to the person using the library?

I can put a PR up if this feature seems like something that people would be interested in, I just have a little touching up to do.

issue_title: Recommended Pipfile.lock usage seems to conflict with certain use-cases; contradicts experience of using Bundler + Gemfile.lock
issue_body:
Reading #2765 , I considered making a comment there, but I think this is really a distinct issue.

Docs state that one should not commit `Pipfile.lock` to your VCS: that appears to conflict with trying to use `pipenv` to get reproducible deployments and testing.
Perhaps I'm misunderstanding the expected `pipenv` usage, but having used `bundler` in the past, it's worth noting that `bundler` explicitly recommends committing `Gemfile.lock`.

Bundler provides a [pretty good explanation](https://bundler.io/v1.3/rationale.html#checking-your-code-into-version-control) of [why its valuable](https://bundler.io/v1.3/rationale.html#sharing-your-application-with-other-developers) to [put the `Gemfile.lock` into version control](https://bundler.io/v1.3/rationale.html#a-simple-bundler-workflow). Why does that reasoning not apply to `pipenv`?
Is there a philosophical gulf between `pipenv` and `bundler` at play? If I _do_ want to record my `Pipfile.lock`, where is that going to let me down?

Note that the Ruby community has been pretty successful with Bundler so far -- that's the justification for following their lead on `dev` requirements (and I buy it! I may not love everything Ruby, but it seems to work fine for that community). So it seems that there needs to be a pretty strong justification for breaking from their typical practice here.

My team is currently committing our lockfiles as we start using `pipenv`, and using `pipenv install --ignore-pipfile`. I'd really like more clarity -- either here or in the docs -- on how `pipenv` expects to be used to produce simple, reproducible builds if the `Pipfile.lock` is never recorded.

# Use Cases

I was able to think of a few simple scenarios in which it's clear to me why you want `Pipfile.lock` in the git repo.
I'm sure there are others too, these are just the ones most familiar to me personally.

If there's a clear answer on how to handle these use-cases with `pipenv`, I'd love to know (and to change what I and my coworkers are doing as we start trying out `pipenv` usage).

## 1: simple deployment with pipenv

- wsgi app in a git repo
- servers `git clone` the repo, `pipenv ...` to setup a virtualenv, and then serve the app with a wsgi server
- should be able to deploy old commits easily, even if there are newer versions of packages
- the servers should all use the same package versions
- if pipenv commands fail, try to recover, and if they keep failing, abort the whole deployment and the new servers never go live

## 2: simple (less risky) deployment with pipenv

same as above except...
- after install, server is turned into an image (e.g. AWS AMI, Docker Container, etc)
- real servers spin up from that image

Note that we _can_ just install from the `Pipfile` in this case, but it would mean that we have less control over when we update dependencies.

## 3: git-bisect with reliable results

- a bug is found in an app
- a simple test for the bug is devised
- apply that test to see where the bug was introduced with `git bisect`, using `Pipfile.lock` to guarantee that we don't break past versions of the code by introducing newer-than-expected libraries

issue_title: Repeatability: offline mirror
issue_body:
The lockfile goes a long way towards reliable and repeatable builds. One reason that pipenv does not yet provide a 100% repeatability guarantee is its dependency on remote resources, mainly package repositories. If a package is removed from the online repository, pipenv will not be able to install from the lockfile! Yarn is listed as one of the inspirers of the pipenv project, and so I propose to implement their solution to this issue!

You can read about the “Offline Mirror” feature on their blog and in their docs:

https://yarnpkg.com/blog/2016/11/24/offline-mirror/

I think it should be possible for pipenv with relatively simple changes. The Pipfile can include a feature toggle and an option to specify the directory, by default a directory within the current directory. Pipenv can configure pip’s cache directory for the duration of the pipenv invocation to make it work!

The more complicated component of this feature is managing the cache folder. I _think_ it should be managed by pipenv to always match the lockfile. That means it should only include the tarballa for the packages in the lockfile and nothing else. This means you can check in the folder into source control (perhaps using git-lfs if filesize is prohibitive), to reach the 100% repeatability goal!

What do you think of this proposal? Can we make it work? How can I help?

issue_title: '--completion' should take an argument on which shell to eval for
issue_body:
##### Expected result

`pipenv` should be usable with [`direnv`](https://github.com/direnv/direnv), i.e. like [`minikube`](https://github.com/kubernetes/minikube) does:

```
eval "$(minikube docker-env --shell bash)"
```
the `shell` parameter exactly does what i would expect for `pipenv`.

##### Actual result

internally, `direnv` uses `bash` spawn to do it's work, and will pass the changes to the actual shell.
therefore, using `direnv` in conjunction with `fish` shell will always detect `fish` as shell.
as the syntax is not compatible, this will fail.

##### Steps to replicate

* use `direnv`
* have `eval "$(pipenv --completion)"` in `.envrc`
* issue `direnv allow`

issue_title: virtualenv hangs while creating env near big .html files
issue_body:
I've stumbled upon the fact that ``virtualenv`` just hang when I was creating an environment in my ``~/tmp`` dir. Looking at ``top`` output I found out that ``pip`` process (or more precisely ``python -c 'import sys, pip; sys.exit(pip.main....``) was eating ~100% of CPU and a lot of memory (GBs).

Digging into the issue I found out that pip was parsing huge (if you call 64 Mb huge) ``.html`` file I had in ``~/tmp`` and I think was trying to determine its encoding. That process took a lot of CPU time which looked like it just hang.

I wondered why would pip parse arbitrary ``.html`` files in current directory. After stepping out of ~~rabbit hole~~ stack, I found out that for some reason ``virtualenv`` passes current dir in ``find_links`` to ``pip``.

It looks like this ``'.'`` has been in code since forever, but I can't find (didn't dig deep enough) the reason for it. We don't want to install stuff from current dir, do we? I think we should remove everything except ``virtualenv_support`` dir from ``file_search_dirs`` method since everything we want to install is in that dir anyway.

issue_title: stamp 0uL with mix is removed
issue_body:
Currently, you are required to specify a volume greater than 0uL even if you want to use the stamp operation only to mix.

This is because, if you specify 0uL, the stamp operation is removed from the autoprotocol output.

e.g. the following will not produce a stamp operation in the resulting autoprotocol but it should.

```
p = Protocol()

plate = p.ref("plate1", cont_type="96-pcr", storage="cold_4", discard=False)
wells = get_column_wells(plate, range(1,6))

p.provision_by_name(Reagent.water, wells, ul(160))

p.stamp(wells[0], 
        wells[0],
        ul(0),
        shape={'rows':8, 'columns':5},
        mix_before=True,
        repetitions=20)
```

issue_title: Rename SpatialPooler to SP
issue_body: In `https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/nupic/research` we have a plenty of implementations of spatial and temporal poolers. However, most or all them are inherited from two super classes: `SpatialPooler` (https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/nupic/research/spatial_pooler.py) and `TP` (https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/nupic/research/TP.py). Today, someone has to do the following in her code if she wants work directly with them: `from nupic.research.spatial_pooler import SpatialPooler as SP` `from nupic.research.TP import TP` I think all here agree that these classes with different conventions are terribly ugly and weird are they are conceptually related.. This done, we would have two nice super classes with fully pure implementation of CLA in Python which will avoid a lot of confusion for who wish get started: `SP` (https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/nupic/research/sp.py) `TP` (https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/nupic/research/tp.py) Please, don't extend this discussion to other implementations of SP and TP for now. On the contrary, we'll have longer discussion about concepts and preferences.. This is a issue very simple to solve. So please, let's focus only on what I'm proposing.. Update: I changed my mind as `sp` and `tp` suffixes and prefixes are everywhere in code and this helps decrease the lenght of file names in same way that `opf_*`, `cla_*` does. In this case, is better just do the inverse, i.e. rename `SpatialPooler` to `SP` than rename `TP` to `TemporalPooler`. In a future issue, we could think in renaming to SP and TP to SpatialPooler and TemporalPooler respectivelly, but not yet, as this involves big changes in code..

issue_title: Centralize all .capnp files to same directory
issue_body:
The .capnp files must maintain their directory structures relative to each other. To simplify this, we can just put them all in `src/nupic/proto/` and have them use relative references to other files in the same directory.

The alternative is to leave the files distributed throughout the directories in nupic.core and maintain the directory stucture in the clients (in nupic the paths would be like `nupic/bindings/algorithms/SpatialPoolerProto.capnp`. This is nice for nupic.core but requires clients to understand the nupic.core directory structure and it creates longer paths.

issue_title: RFC Generic BibTeX export
issue_body:
Originally on 2011-06-06

Currently **BibTeX** export is generated out of the box, by using a single *output format*, calling a single *format element*, namely `bfe_bibtex.py`. This imply the business-logic of the BibTeX export is all hard-coded in one place and can not easily be adapted to the most different document type. This has an impact, e.g. in CDS, when exporting Thesis documents.

One possible solution would be to have generic `bfe_bibtex_field.py`, based on `bfe_bibtex.format_bibtex_field`, able to format certain subfields, and to provide demo BibTeX exports, that are adapted to the specific document type.

issue_title: BTrees should support the PURE_PYTHON environment variable at runtime
issue_body:
This has become common for zopefoundation packages such as [persistent](https://github.com/zopefoundation/persistent/blob/master/persistent/__init__.py#L21) [Acquisition](https://github.com/zopefoundation/Acquisition/blob/master/src/Acquisition/__init__.py#L945), [zope.security](https://github.com/zopefoundation/zope.security/blob/master/src/zope/security/proxy.py#L383) and so on. It's convenient for debugging and exploring the system, and also for testing.

BTrees does *not* support this variable, and this causes problems because it conflicts with `persistent`, which does:
```console
$ pip freeze | egrep  "BTree|pers"
BTrees==4.4.1
persistent==4.2.4.2
$ cat test.py
import persistent
from BTrees.OOBTree import OOBTree

class BaseClass(persistent.Persistent):
    pass

class MyTree(OOBTree, BaseClass):
    pass
$ python --version
Python 3.6.2
$ python test.py # C Extensions work
$ PURE_PYTHON=1 python test.py # Mixed does not
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 7, in <module>
    class MyTree(OOBTree, BaseClass):
TypeError: multiple bases have instance lay-out conflict
```

If this is agreeable, I can work up a PR when I get some time.

issue_title: Document how to use DictionaryField so it does not get picked up in "undesired scenarios"
issue_body:
In DictionaryField datamanager docstring it says:(https://github.com/zopefoundation/z3c.form/blob/3.2.7/src/z3c/form/datamanager.py#L110-L114) 

    NOTE: Even though, this data manager allows nearly all kinds of
    mappings, by default it is only registered for dict, because it
    would otherwise get picked up in undesired scenarios. If you want
    to it use for another mapping, register the appropriate adapter in
    your application.

What does this mean? Why would it be not safe to register DictionaryField datamanager for PersistentDict (handy for using z3c.form with annotations)? Would it then be better to subclass PersistentDict and register DictionaryField for that (makes things potentionally brittle though)?

issue_title: pkg_resources causes a slow import; any way to avoid?
issue_body:
The use of pkg_resources.parse_version (see #162) causes the import time of numexpr to slow down significantly, from approx 0.4 seconds without pkg_resources.parse_version to 2.1 seconds with pkg_resources.parse_version.

(perhaps I should note that I am not a direct consumer of numexpr and have never used it, but pandas does and I use pandas)

Please consider alternate methods... if there are any.

The culprit is in expressions.py

```
import numpy
from pkg_resources import parse_version
_np_version = parse_version(numpy.__version__)
```

(my "speedup" was to comment out those last two lines referencing parse_version and substitute `_np_version = '1.12.1'` just to see how this impacts import time)

issue_title: wrap std::min + std::max in () to avoid issues building brotli on windows
issue_body:
windef.h (included by windows.h) defines min+max macros, so to avoid compilation issues on windows whenever include brotli headers after including windows headers (which can be very tricky to avoid/workaround in some projects), its best to either:
- convert lines like std::min(a,b) to (std::min)(a,b), or
- keep such code out of the headers that get pulled in when including encode.h / decode.h

issue_title: Introduce concept of a "keyed" Frame
issue_body:
This is the first step in implementing joins (#1080).

The notion of "key" exists in many data frame implementations: it's primary key in SQL, an index in pandas DataFrame, key in R data.table, or row.names in R data.frame. These concepts denote slightly different things across implementations; we want to implement "key"s with the following properties:
* A key is optional: each frame may or may not have a key.
* A key is a regular column within the frame (it is merely marked as a key). A key can also be comprised of several columns (to be implemented later).
* Key column(s) must be the first column(s) in the frame. When a column is marked as a key, that column is moved to the front of the frame if it is not there already.
* If a frame has a key, this information should be preserved when saving in `.jay` format.
* When a keyed frame is displayed, the key column should be visually separated from the rest of the columns and should replace the usual row numbers.
* The values in the key column must be unique.
* The key column must either be sorted or hashed or both (hashing to be implemented later).
* If a key is set on a frame, then the row-selector expression such as `frame[i, ...]` will now refer to the values in the key column. For example, if the key column is of string type, then `i` can be a string, or a list of strings, or a slice of strings. Likewise, if the key column is integer, then integer `i`s refer to row values rather than row numbers (i.e. `frame[0, :]` is no longer the first row, but rather the row where the key column has value `0`).
* Keyed frames are eligible for performing natural joins: if `df0` is keyed and `df1` contains a column with the same name/type as `df0`'s key, then the expression `df1[df0, ...]` is understood as selecting all rows from `df1` and matching rows from `df0` where matching is performed according to the values in the `df0`'s key column.

issue_title: memoization behaviour prevents memory to be released.
issue_body:
memoization in NeuroM involves a class variable: https://github.com/BlueBrain/NeuroM/blob/master/neurom/utils.py#L44
When accessing properties on a lot of morphologies sequentially, even though we don't reference the morphology anymore, properties are still stored in that cache.
The consequence is that memory keeps growing even if we actually needs only 1 morphology at a time. This prevents analyzing large number of morphologies.
Please make that memoization optional or attach the cache to the morphology.

issue_title: Type coercion
issue_body:
### Description
In some cases front end could pass integer as strings (especially old legacy code and it's hard to quickly change that behaviour). 

Could type coercion be introduced to convert that values to correct type internally like [Cerberus](http://docs.python-cerberus.org/en/stable/) do?

```python
"amount": {'type': 'float', 'coerce': float}
```

Example:

Swagger:
```yaml
...
parameters:
      - name: "amount"
        in: "body"
        description: "amount of something"
        required: true
        type: "number"
        format: "float"
```

Input
```python
{"amount": "103.25"}
```

Becomes
```python
{"amount": 103.25}
```
Validation is passed

issue_title: Parameters order for Error Exception Class
issue_body:
Based on [pull request 51](https://github.com/cenobites/flask-jsonrpc/pull/51) which allows setting a value for "data"...

The actual parameters order for Error Class is:

`def __init__(self, message=None, code=None, data=None):`

In order to set a value for "data" without setting the "message", we must adjust our code and use a named argument:

`raise InvalidParamsError(data='This value cannot be an integer.')`

But this module (flask-jsonrpc) still raises exceptions using positioned arguments, like: 

`InvalidRequestError('The method you are trying to access is not availble by GET requests')`

It sets 'The method you are trying to access is not availble by GET requests' to `message` instead of `data`.

```
"data": null,
"message": "The method you are trying to access is not availble by GET requests",
```

While the correct should be:

```
"data": "The method you are trying to access is not availble by GET requests",
"message": "Invalid Request.",
```

So the problem is: should this module use named argument for setting `data` or should the Error class change the parameters order to `data` come first to `messsage` for the first argument be always a `data` by default?

issue_title: map function considerably slower than pythons built-in multiprocessing map
issue_body:
I am not sure if this is an issue, but I suppose I will submit nevertheless. I have been porting some code from python's multiprocessing to pathos mainly due to pickling issue. However, it seems that pathos's map function is considerably slower than pythons built-in multiprocessing map. Consider to the following simple function:

    def foo(a):
        return 2*a

    In [136]: %timeit python_pool.map(foo,range(100))
    1000 loops, best of 3: 360 µs per loop

    In [137]: %timeit pathos_pool.map(foo,range(100))
    100 loops, best of 3: 8.2 ms per loop

    In [138]: %timeit [foo(i) for i in range(100)]
    100000 loops, best of 3: 9.83 µs per loop

for bigger arrays:

    In [139]: %timeit python_pool.map(foo,range(100000))
    10 loops, best of 3: 23.1 ms per loop

    In [140]: %timeit pathos_pool.map(foo,range(100000))
    1 loops, best of 3: 883 ms per loop
    
For more complicated functions, map performs even worse. Is this expected? I am guessing the serialisation mechanism is different, but this is a huge overhead to pay for. I am using the git version of pathos
 
    pip2 install git+https://github.com/uqfoundation/pathos.git@master

issue_title: CFFI archives on pypi not compatible with OSX
issue_body:
If pants' own requirements are resolved against pypi, they will (occasionally?) result in `cp27mu` archives (whereas OSX usually requires `cp27m`, according to @kwlzn).

This results in errors like the following:
```
E       ImportError: dlopen(src/pants/.pants.d/pyprep/requirements/CPython-2.7.10/c922e92ef56a87b960fbf90cacf8ed24e1d1b7c9/.deps/cffi-1.7.0-cp27-cp27mu-macosx_10_10_x86_64.whl/_cffi_backend.so, 2): Symbol not found: _PyUnicodeUCS4_AsASCIIString
E         Referenced from: src/pants/.pants.d/pyprep/requirements/CPython-2.7.10/c922e92ef56a87b960fbf90cacf8ed24e1d1b7c9/.deps/cffi-1.7.0-cp27-cp27mu-macosx_10_10_x86_64.whl/_cffi_backend.so
E         Expected in: flat namespace
E        in src/pants/.pants.d/pyprep/requirements/CPython-2.7.10/c922e92ef56a87b960fbf90cacf8ed24e1d1b7c9/.deps/cffi-1.7.0-cp27-cp27mu-macosx_10_10_x86_64.whl/_cffi_backend.so
```
... note: `cp27mu`.

A workaround is to nuke the
```
~/.cache/pants/python_cache
```
directory, and then retry the resolve.

----

The full solution likely involves adding support for filtering ABIs via https://github.com/pantsbuild/pex/issues/303

issue_body:
I've encountered the case where the generated coveragerc looks like this:

```
[run]
branch = True
timid = True

[report]
exclude_lines =
        def __repr__
        raise NotImplementedError

[paths]

                /usr/local/google/home/tanin/projects/clusterfuzz-tools/shared

error =
                error
                .pants.d/pyprep/sources/7612f833acaeb440ee7d4249c5c2c340885c1ec7
                /usr/local/google/home/tanin/projects/clusterfuzz-tools/error
                /usr/local/google/home/tanin/projects/clusterfuzz-tools/.pants.d/pyprep/sources/7612f833acaeb440ee7d4249c5c2c340885c1ec7
```

(The content is generated from this line: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/backend/python/tasks2/pytest_run.py#L210)

The run command is: `./pants test.pytest --coverage=auto error:test -- -p no:logging`

And here's the BUILD:

```
python_tests(
    name='test',
    sources=rglobs('tests/*.py'),
    coverage='error',
    compatibility=['>=2.7','<3'],
    dependencies=[
        ':src',
        '//shared:test_libs',
        '//3rdparty/python:pyfakefs',
        '//3rdparty/python:mock'
    ]
)
```

`:src` and `//shared:test_libs` are regular `python_library`s. `:src` has the module `error`.

This leads to the generated report below:

```
18:05:50 00:03       [coverage]
                     Name                           Stmts   Miss Branch BrPart  Cover
                     ----------------------------------------------------------------
                     shared/test_libs/__init__.py       0      0      0      0   100%
                     shared/test_libs/helpers.py       38     38      8      0     0%
                     ----------------------------------------------------------------
                     TOTAL                             38     38      8      0     0%
```

You can see that it doesn't include the module `error`. If I remove `//shared:test_libs` from the BUILD target, it suddenly shows the coverage from the module `error`.

After testing, there's something strange around `[paths]`. I'm not sure.

This is Pants 1.3.0.

issue_title: Avoid using `null` on string-based fields such as `CharField`
issue_body:
<!--
Instructions:
 * Fill out the sections below, replace …'s with information about your issue
 * Use the 'preview' function above this text box to verify formatting before submitting
-->

### Observed behavior
There are a few model fields definitions for string-based fields using `null=True` option which is recommended to be avoided by the [Django documentation](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.0/ref/models/fields/#django.db.models.Field.null):

> Avoid using null on string-based fields such as CharField and TextField. If a string-based field has null=True, that means it has two possible values for “no data”: NULL, and the empty string. In most cases, it’s redundant to have two possible values for “no data;” the Django convention is to use the empty string, not NULL. One exception is when a CharField has both unique=True and blank=True set. In this situation, null=True is required to avoid unique constraint violations when saving multiple objects with blank values.

As far as I can see, examples of the above mentioned definitions are contained within:
- `kolibri/models/models.py`
- `kolibri/models/legacy_models.py`

specifically:
- `kolibri/models/models.py`
  - `ContentNode`
    - `license_name = models.CharField(max_length=50, null=True, blank=True)`
    - `license_description = models.CharField(max_length=400, null=True, blank=True)`
    - `description = models.CharField(max_length=400, blank=True, null=True)`
  - `Language`
    - `lang_subcode = models.CharField(max_length=10, db_index=True, blank=True, null=True)`
    - `lang_name = models.CharField(max_length=100, blank=True, null=True)`

- `kolibri/models/legacy_models.py`
  - `License`
    - `license_description = models.CharField(max_length=400, null=True, blank=True)`

### Expected behavior
Remove `null=True` option from the `CharField` model field definitions.


### User-facing consequences
This is primarily a backend related issue with no real consequences to the end users. That being said, it is possible that "emptiness" of the certain field's values is not being checked correctly if they're not taking into account the two possible states for "no data": `NULL` and empty string.

### Errors and logs
There are no errors and logs related to this issue.

### Steps to reproduce
Simply take a look at the codebase, see **Observed behavior** section for the exact files.

### Context
 * Kolibri version: all versions from 0.2.0 forward (as far as I could track)

issue_title: `django-mptt` dependency missing from Kolibri setup
issue_body:
## Issue

In the [setup instructions](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri), `pip install -e .` is used to install Django and other dependencies. However running `kolibri manage runserver` on a fresh installations gives:

```
vagrant@kolibri ~> kolibri manage runserver
Unhandled exception in thread started by <function wrapper at 0x7fba4bb75938>
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/django/utils/autoreload.py", line 226, in wrapper
    fn(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/django/core/management/commands/runserver.py", line 109, in inner_run
    autoreload.raise_last_exception()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/django/utils/autoreload.py", line 249, in raise_last_exception
    six.reraise(*_exception)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/django/utils/autoreload.py", line 226, in wrapper
    fn(*args, **kwargs)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/django/__init__.py", line 18, in setup
    apps.populate(settings.INSTALLED_APPS)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/django/apps/registry.py", line 108, in populate
    app_config.import_models(all_models)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/django/apps/config.py", line 202, in import_models
    self.models_module = import_module(models_module_name)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/importlib/__init__.py", line 37, in import_module
    __import__(name)
  File "/kolibri/kolibri/auth/models.py", line 28, in <module>
    from mptt.models import MPTTModel, TreeForeignKey
ImportError: No module named mptt.models
^Cfish: Job 1, “kolibri manage runserver” terminated by signal SIGINT (Quit request from job control (^C))
```

## Work-around

The correct dependencies can be installed using

    pip install -r requirements.txt

## Analysis

If we're using  `pip install -e .` as the canonical way to install Kolibri's dependencies, all required packages should be included in the `install_requires` field in `setup.py`.

We should clarify how these differ from the dependencies listed the various requirements files, and potentially clean these up if there is redundancy.

issue_title: Stopping and starting the ipfs daemon from the interface does not correctly update the running status
issue_body:
From the main replay page, a user may stop the IPFS daemon by clicking a button. When doing so, the button becomes disabled and the status message changes until a polling process shows the 
 IPFS daemon status has changed. Once complete, the statuses are updated.

When restarting the daemon using this same method, the status button disables, the status changes, and the daemon is started. However, the check for the daemon status shows that it is not running on the ipwb CLI and thus, once the button is enabled again, the status message says the daemon is still not running.

Whatever method is being used to check if the daemon is running might be checking prematurely, seeing the daemon is not running, and reporting as such regardless of the ultimate status once the daemon start procedure has completed.

issue_title: Improve naming of ctypes_library and library
issue_body:
[ctypes_library.py](https://github.com/ni/nimi-python/blob/master/generated/nidmm/ctypes_library.py) contains a class called NidmmCtypesLibrary with 1 to 1 correspondence to the driver library functions.

[library.py](https://github.com/ni/nimi-python/blob/master/generated/nidmm/library.py) contains LibrarySingleton class and a couple of functions that *should* be private.

I propose:

* Rename NidmmCtypesLibrary to Library
    * Matches Session member name
    * Simpler (no codegen magic in class name)
* Rename library.py to library_singleton.py
* Rename  ctypes_library.py to library.py
* Move get_library_name() and get_library_type() to inside the class and pre-pend with _ (private)

I realized how we can reduce confusion on this while writing an overview of our testing strategy.

issue_title: Log queries to the demo somewhere
Add a flag which avoids using the validation data for creating a vocabulary.
issue_body: Hi, I was looking at the code for `train` command and noticed that the vocabulary is built from all datasets (train, dev, test). Is this intentional? I am asking because I thought the common thing to do is to build vocabulary from train set only so that words in dev/test set that does not occur in train set are converted into `<unk>` and given a single embedding. Building vocabulary from all three sets means that there will be no unknown words in dev/test set (assuming `min_count=1`).

issue_title: unlabeled test set cannot be included in training vocabulary
issue_body:
In the "Kaggle competition" use case, the test dataset has no labels. This means that if you try to specify `test_data_path` (e.g. in order to get the embeddings for that vocabulary), the trainer will crash with

```

```

I was able to workaround this by having the `DatasetReader` generate fake labels, but that's not a good solution.

# Overview

Across all libraries we provide consistent validation interface:

```python
# Raise on invalid
dp = DataPackage(invalid_descriptor) # raise list of errors

# Non-strict mode
dp = DataPackage(invalid_descriptor, strict=False)
dp.valid # False
dp.errors # list of errors

# Validate function
validate(invalid_descriptor) # raise list of errors
```

# Tasks

- [ ] sync implementation with described interface
- [ ] sync public `Profile` class with described interface
- [ ] review updating system to work with validation system

issue_title: Improve behavior of error reporting
issue_body:
Currently by default running DSC on a local computer hides stderr and stdout. We should write them to files and point users to them when there are errors. We need to also clean them up if they turn out to be empty to prevent leaving behind us a trail of trivial err and out files. 

Also we need to make the rerun prompt `Rscript <path to script>` more obvious -- even suggest in the prompt that users should open up the file and interact with it.

issue_title: The different actions between BinaryTrie and HexaryTrie
issue_body:
* Version: 1.1.0 or latest commit (4b78035f0dc1314cbda3c58070ae1fe074fe08a1)
* Python: 3.6
* OS: osx

### What was wrong?
When I was trying to switch the transaction and receipt trie from `HexaryTrie` to `BinaryTrie` for `ShardingVM` (https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/issues/331), I found that the APIs results of these two tries are slightly different in dealing *not-found-in-dict*.

`HexaryTrie` returns `BLANK_NODE`
https://github.com/ethereum/py-trie/blob/4b78035f0dc1314cbda3c58070ae1fe074fe08a1/trie/hexary.py#L69-L79

`BinaryTrie` returns `None` 
https://github.com/ethereum/py-trie/blob/4b78035f0dc1314cbda3c58070ae1fe074fe08a1/trie/binary.py#L57-L86

### What did you expect it to do?
I replaced `None` with `BLANK_NODE` in `trie._get()` function,  and then the tests are passed.

#### Code to reproduce the error
```python
from trie import (
    BinaryTrie,
    HexaryTrie,
)


hexary_trie = HexaryTrie(db={})
assert b'hello' not in hexary_trie

binary_trie = BinaryTrie(db={})
assert b'hello' not in binary_trie
```

result: 
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "hello.py", line 16, in <module>
    assert b'hello' not in binary_trie
AssertionError
```
^^^^ That's false.

I'm afraid that I may break `BinaryTrie`, so I wanna ask @NIC619, could I simply I replace `None` with `BLANK_NODE` in `trie._get()` function?

issue_title: Remove non-standard tag from wgn filename
issue_body:
Currently, Wagon appends two additional fields to each archive's file name if running on Linux - distribution name and distribution codename. These tags are not a part of the wheel standard and are only there to help with installing on Linux (if not all wheels are manylinux1). 

To accommodate the need to still identify the Linux distribution and release the wagon was created on, these tags should be removed from the file name and remain in the metadata only (where they also currently reside). Once manylinux1 is (hopefully) easier to generate, we would be able to deprecate this information from the metadata as well.

Practically, this means that if you want to auto-identify on which distribution and release you most probably can install the wagon, you would have to either use the CLI or the API to retrieve its metadata and read the relevant fields.

issue_title: Bad package that breaks /bin will trip up conda
issue_body:
A build script like this will break conda, when it tries to link "conda" into the bin directory.  This script accidentally copies the file into a file named bin and not into a directory bin.  This builds successfully, but breaks conda when installing.
```
gcc -O -o pstree pstree.c
cp pstree $PREFIX/bin
```

We should test for this since it is a common and easy error to make.
